title: Psalms 21
subtitle: The King Rejoices in God’s Strength

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “You Have Granted Him His Heart’s Desire”

heb.paragraph: "The king rejoices in your strength (ʿōz), LORD" (v.1). A thanksgiving for answered prayer. Psalm 20 asked God to answer the king; Psalm 21 celebrates that he did. The pair: petition → fulfilment.

refs.note: The companion petition psalm. Ps 20 asks; Ps 21 thanks.
David takes the crown of the Ammonite king — the possible historical setting.

notes.note: "The king rejoices in your strength. You have granted him his heart’s desire." The answered prayer: Psalm 20’s petition fulfilled.
"He asked you for life, and you gave it to him — length of days, for ever and ever." The life-request goes beyond natural lifespan — "for ever and ever" points to resurrection hope.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "a crown of pure gold" is both literal (royal crown) and metaphorical (divine favour as adornment).
Calvin: "length of days for ever and ever" transcends David. Calvin: the Davidic promises find their eternal fulfilment only in Christ’s unending reign.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "for ever and ever" (ʿōlām wā-ʿed) is the strongest possible expression of enduring life. It points beyond any mortal king.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "for ever and ever" exceeds any historical king’s lifespan. The psalm speaks beyond David to the Messiah whose reign never ends.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: Psalm 21 is the thanksgiving that follows Psalm 20’s intercession. The liturgical pair teaches: pray before battle, give thanks after.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "you have made him glad with the joy of your presence" — the king’s greatest blessing is not military victory but divine presence. Joy comes from God’s face, not from the battlefield.

hist.context: The king’s thanksgiving for God’s blessings: answered prayers (v.2), rich blessings (v.3), crown of pure gold (v.3), life requested and granted (v.4), splendour and majesty (v.5), eternal blessings (v.6), gladness through God’s presence (v.7).

sections.header: Verses 8–13 — “Your Hand Will Lay Hold on All Your Enemies”

heb.paragraph: "You will make them like a blazing furnace (ʾēsh) when you appear" (v.9). The second half turns from thanksgiving to judgment on enemies. God’s presence is a furnace to the rebellious. The king’s enemies are consumed; the king’s God exalted.

refs.note: "The day is coming; it will burn like a furnace" — the same furnace-judgment imagery.
The returning Christ who strikes down nations — the eschatological fulfilment.

notes.note: "You will make them like a blazing furnace." God’s presence is fire to the rebellious. The enemies’ destruction is total.
"Be exalted in your strength, LORD." The psalm circles back to v.1 (the king rejoices in God’s strength). The frame is worship.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the furnace image is theophanic — God’s appearance itself is the fire that consumes. Presence and judgment are the same event.
Calvin: "be exalted in your strength" — the prayer for God’s strength to be visible. Calvin: God’s power is always real; the prayer is for it to be manifest.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "like a blazing furnace" is literally "like a furnace of fire at the time of your face" — God’s face IS the fire. The theophany is the judgment.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the psalm ends in corporate worship: "we will sing." The king’s personal thanksgiving becomes the nation’s hymn.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the judgment section is not vindictiveness but confidence in divine justice. The enemies plotted evil (v.11); God ensures their plots fail.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "we will sing" shifts from the king’s voice to the community’s. The psalm’s ending is choral, not solo.

hist.context: God’s right hand finds all enemies (v.8). They will be destroyed as in a fiery furnace (v.9), their posterity blotted out (v.10). Their plots fail because God sends them fleeing (vv.11–12). The psalm closes: "Be exalted in your strength, LORD; we will sing and praise your might" (v.13). From the king’s joy to the congregation’s worship.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: Source of the king's strength and victory (vv.1-7,13)
ppl.text: Yahweh gives the king 'his heart's desire' (v.2) and 'blessings of good things' (v.3) — language recalling the Davidic covenant promises of 2 Samuel 7. The 'crown of pure gold' (v.3) symbolizes God-given royal authority. Verse 7 grounds royal success in covenant loyalty (chesed): the king stands firm because he trusts Elyon (the Most High), not military power.

ppl.name: The King
ppl.text: Unlike Psalm 20's pre-battle prayer, Psalm 21 is the post-battle thanksgiving. The king 'rejoices in your strength' (v.1) — his power is derivative, not self-generated. The 'length of days forever and ever' (v.4) points beyond any single monarch's lifespan, giving this psalm a messianic trajectory. Early Christian readers saw Christ's resurrection as the ultimate fulfillment of this eternal-life language.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author and likely the king being described
ppl.text: The superscription assigns this to David. Read alongside Psalm 20, it forms a liturgical pair: the congregation prays for the king before battle (Ps 20) and celebrates Yahweh's answer afterward (Ps 21). The shift from petition to praise models the prayer-thanksgiving rhythm central to Davidic worship.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVWhen you appear for battle, you will burn them up as in a blazing furnace. The Lord will swallow them up in his wrath, and his fire will consume them.ESVYou will make them as a blazing oven when you appear. The LORD will swallow them up in his wrath, and fire will consume them.NIVThrough the victories you gave, his glory is great; you have bestowed on him splendor and majesty.ESVHis glory is great through your salvation; splendor and majesty you bestow on him.

rec.text: ""The king rejoices in your strength. You have granted him his heart’s desire." The answered prayer: Psalm 20’s petition fulfilled."

rows.label: Judgment — 21:8–13
rows.text: Enemies like a furnace. Plots fail. "Be exalted in your strength, LORD."

hebtext.note: "The king rejoices in your strength (ʿōz), LORD" (v.1). A thanksgiving for answered prayer. Psalm 20 asked God to answer the king; Psalm 21 celebrates that he did. The pair: petition → fulfilment.

"You will make them like a blazing furnace (ʾēsh) when you appear" (v.9). The second half turns from thanksgiving to judgment on enemies. God’s presence is a furnace to the rebellious. The king’s enemies are consumed; the king’s God exalted.

themes.note: Sovereignty: God grants the king’s desire and destroys enemies. Judgment: furnace fire. Worship: the psalm frames victory in praise.
